english: 1. to think, to meditate, dgóṅs-pa-la jug-pa (dgongs pa la 'jug pa) to enter into meditation Glr.; di snyám-du dgóṅs-par gyur-to ('di snyam du dgongs par gyur to) he thought so in his mind Dzl.; rgyál-po khoṅ-ráṅ yin dgóṅs-nas (rgyal po khong rang yin dgongs nas) the king thinking that he himself was meant, referring the allusion to himself Glr.; to regard as, bu daṅ drá-bar dgóṅs-pa (bu dang dra bar dgongs pa) to treat one like a son Dzl.; to remember, to think of, to devise, mṅa-ris-kyi vdúl-bya-la (mnga' ris kyi 'dul bya la) remembering those of Nari that were to be converted, thinking of the conversion of Nari Glr.; also with pyir (phyir) Pth.; ṅá-la tugs-brtsé-bar dgóṅs-śig (nga la thugs brtse bar dgongs shig) remember me graciously, frq.; so in a similar manner: to hear graciously, to take a kind interest, share, or concern in, to interest one's self for, to try to promote; so our Lama explained the passage Glr. 101, 9: saṅs-rgyás-kyi bstáṅ-pa-la dgóṅs-nas = bstáṅ-pa pél-bai pyir bsám-blo btáṅ-nas (sangs rgyas kyi bstan pa la dgongs nas = bstan pa 'phel ba'i phyir bsam blo btang nas); to intend, to purpose, with the termin. of the inf., frq., tugs-kyis ma dgóṅs-so (thugs kyis ma dgongs so) he did not intend, he had no mind Pth. - 2. to die, més-kyi dgóṅs-dus-kyi mchód-pa btsug (mes kyi dgongs dus kyi mchod pa btsug) Glr. is stated to mean: he instituted sacrifices for the remembrance of his grandfather's death; and so similarly in other passages. II. sbst., also tugs-dgóṅs (thugs dgongs), 1. the act of thinking, meditating, pondering, tugs-dgóṅs gtóṅ-ba (thugs dgongs gtong ba) Mil. to meditate; thought, rgyál-poi túgs-kyi dgóṅs-pa-la 'gáṅ-du pyin' snyám-pa (rgyal po'i thugs kyi dgongs pa la 'gang du phyin' snyam pa) lit. in the king's 'mind-thoughts' was thought: where shall I go? Glr.; meaning, sense, esp. the sense of sacred words or writings, therefore dgóṅs-pa grél-ba (dgongs pa 'grel ba) to explain that sense, dgóṅs-grél (dgongs 'grel), dgóṅs-bgról (dgongs bgrol) commentary; a will, a wish, rgyál-poi (or -pos) dgóṅs-pa bźin-du bsgrub nús-so (rgyal po'i (or -pos) dgongs pa bzhin du bsgrub nus so) I am able to fulfil your majesty's wish Dzl.; skyóṅ-bai dgóṅs-pa-chan (skyong ba'i dgongs pa can) Glr. 104, poetically, one having the desire of protecting, one wishing to protect. - 2. soul, dgóṅs-pa mya-ṅán-las dás-so (dgongs pa mya ngan las 'das so) his soul quitted (the abode of) misery. - 3. permission C., W., *góṅ-pa żú-wa* (dgongs pa zhu ba) to beg leave, to ask permission, *góṅ-pa táṅ-wa* (dgongs pa gtong ba), resp. *náṅ-wa* (gnang ba) to give permission, in Sik. also: to grant admission; but gen. it is used for leave of absence, and *kó-la góṅ-pog lhuṅ soṅ* (kho la dgongs pog lhung song) C. signifies: he has been dismissed, turned out.

1. to laugh, Glr.; gen. in such expressions as the following *go'-dhó* (lit. *bro*) *yim-pa* (dgod bro yim pa) C. to make one's self ridiculous, a laughing-stock, also Glr.; *hab-gód có-ce* (hab dgod co ce) W. to set up a loud laugh, to burst out into laughter: dgod-bág (dgod bag) a jest, joke Sch.; cf. bgad-pa (bgad pa). - 2. v. gód-pa (god pa).

1. a solitary place; desert, wilderness, dgón-pai gnas (dgon pa'i gnas) a desolate place or region Stg.; dgon-dúṅ (dgon dung) a sandy desert, sands Sch. (Zam. अरण्य and dgón-pa (dgon pa): कान्तार forest). - 2. hermitage. - 3. monastery, frq.; dgón-pa-pa (dgon pa pa) 1. a man dwelling in a desert, a hermit. 2. a man dwelling in a monastery, a monk; dgon-pa-ma (dgon pa ma) fem.

I. vb. implying necessity, as well as want: to be necessary, to be obliged or compelled; to want, to stand in need of; also where we use 'ought'; it is gen. used with the verbal root or with the termin. of the inf. present, byed dgos (byed dgos), but sometimes also of the inf. future or perfect, e.g. rin-po-ches brtsigs dgós-na rin-po-che méd-pas sa-las bya dgos (rin po ches brtsigs dgos na rin po che med pas sa las bya dgos) though it ought to have been built of precious stones, yet for want of such, it will have to be constructed of earth Dzl. - la (la) gen. denotes the person standing in need of a thing, e.g. ṅá-la dgos (nga la dgos) I want, I stand in need of, but it also refers to the object for which a thing is wanted: rgya-gár-du gró-ba-la gser dgos-pa yin (rgya gar du 'gro ba la gser dgos pa yin) for a journey to India gold is wanted (required); in such a case the termin. may also be used: ci źig-tu dgos (ci zhig tu dgos), for what purpose is it wanted? zas za ma dgos (zas za ma dgos) I did not want to eat Mil.; dgós-pai dús-su blaṅs (dgos pa'i dus su blangs) they took them when they wanted them Glr.; bźéṅs ma dgos (bzhengs ma dgos) he was not obliged to erect ... Glr. - In commanding, the word is used to paraphrase the imperative of a verb: oṅ-bar dgos ('ong bar dgos) come! in entreating, the respectful term is chosen: byon dgos (byon dgos) Mil., or in W.: *skyod dgos żu* (skyod dgos zhu) 'you must come, pray!' =
